name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱللَّـٰتَ وَٱلْعُزَّىٰ (١٩)
```

```english
Have you considered Lat and ‘Uzza? (19)
```

```arabic
وَمَنَوٰةَ ٱلثَّالِثَةَ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰٓ (٢٠)
```

```english
and Manat, the third one? (20)
```

```arabic
أَلَكُمُ ٱلذَّكَرُ وَلَهُ ٱلْأُنثَىٰ (٢١)
```

```english
Are you to have males and He females? (21)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ إِذًا قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزَىٰٓ (٢٢)
```

```english
That, then, will be an unfair division! (22)
```

```arabic
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى ٱلْأَنفُسُ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّن رَّبِّهِمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (٢٣)
```

```english
These are but names, which you have coined—you and your fathers—for which Allah has not sent down any authority. They follow nothing but conjectures and the desires of the [lower] soul, while there has already come to them the guidance from their Lord. (23)
```

```arabic
أَمْ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ مَا تَمَنَّىٰ (٢٤)
```

```english
Shall man have whatever he yearns for? (24)
```

```arabic
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ (٢٥)
```

```english
Yet to Allah belong this world and the Hereafter. (25)
```

```arabic
۞ وَكَم مِّن مَّلَكٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ لَا تُغْنِى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن يَأْذَنَ ٱللَّهُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَرْضَىٰٓ (٢٦)
```

```english
How many an angel there is in the heavens whose intercession is of not any avail, except after Allah permits whomever He wishes and approves of! (26)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَيُسَمُّونَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ تَسْمِيَةَ ٱلْأُنثَىٰ (٢٧)
```

```english
Indeed those who do not believe in the Hereafter give female names to the angels. (27)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ ۖ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ شَيْـًٔا (٢٨)
```

```english
They do not have any knowledge of that. They follow nothing but conjectures, and conjecture is no substitute for the truth. (28)
```

```arabic
فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا (٢٩)
```

```english
So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. (29)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (٣٠)
```

```english
That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are [rightly] guided. (30)
```

```arabic
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ بِٱلْحُسْنَى (٣١)
```

```english
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, that He may requite those who do evil for what they have done, and reward those who do good with the best [of rewards]. (31)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّمَمَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَٰسِعُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰٓ (٣٢)
```

```english
Those who avoid major sins and indecencies, apart from [minor and occasional] lapses. Indeed your Lord is expansive in [His] forgiveness. He knows you best since [the time] He produced you from the earth and since you were foetuses in the bellies of your mothers. So do not flaunt your piety: He knows best those who are Godwary. (32)
```

## (Commentary)

A portion of the verses in the second section of the three sections of this *Surah* addresses the matter of idols and their worship, based on the claim that they will intercede for their devotees. It refutes them with the most eloquent rebuttal and contains an allusion to the matter of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final gathering of souls for judgment* — which is the objective of the third section.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ اللَّاتَ وَالْعُزَّى وَمَناةَ الثَّالِثَةَ الْأُخْرى** **"Have you considered Lat and ‘Uzza, and Manat, the third one besides?"** (53:19-20). Since the preceding verses established the truthfulness of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and that what he brings is a revelation (الوحي) — *the divine communication sent down to a messenger* — being revealed to him, and since this entails the truth of Prophethood (النبوة) based on monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the negation of partners, He branched off from this to discuss the idols: *al-Lat*, *al-‘Uzza*, and *Manat*. According to the polytheists, these were statues representing the angels, based on their claim that the angels are females; or some were for angels and others for humans, as some of them claimed. He negated their lordship (الربوبية) and divinity (الألوهية), as well as the independence of the angels—who are the lords of these idols—in intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — and their alleged femininity. He also pointed toward other truths that lead to the Return and the recompense for deeds.

*Al-Lat*, *al-‘Uzza*, and *Manat* were three idols worshipped by the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). They differed in describing their forms, the locations where they were erected, which Arabs worshipped them, and the reasons that necessitated their worship. These are conflicting reports that cannot be relied upon; the only certainty is what we have mentioned.

The meaning is: If the matter is as we have described regarding the truth of the call and the truthfulness of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his claim of revelation and messengership from Allah, the Glorified, then tell Me about *al-Lat*, *al-‘Uzza*, and *Manat*, which is the third besides the first two and others—those which you claim are idols of the angels, who are the daughters of Allah according to your claim.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَكُمُ الذَّكَرُ وَلَهُ الْأُنْثى تِلْكَ إِذاً قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزى** **"Is it for you the male and for Him the female? That, then, is an unfair division!"** (53:21-22). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) tinged with mockery. An unfair (ضيزى) division means one that is biased and unjust.

The meaning is: If it were so, and the lords of these idols were the angels, the daughters of Allah, while you do not accept for yourselves anything but male children, then is the male for you and the female for Allah, the Glorified? That division, then, is a biased and unjust division—said in mockery.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ** **"These are but names you have coined—you and your fathers—for which Allah has not sent down any authority."** (53:23). The pronoun "these" (هِيَ) refers to *al-Lat*, *al-‘Uzza*, and *Manat*, or to them insofar as they are idols. The pronoun in "you have coined them" (سَمَّيْتُمُوها) refers to the names; "coining names" means designating them as such. What is meant by authority (السلطان) is proof (البرهان).

The meaning is: These idols are not deities; they are merely names that you and your fathers have designated for them. There are no external realities (مصاديق) or named entities (مسميات) behind these names. Allah has not sent down any proof with them by which their lordship or divinity could be demonstrated.

The sum of the verse is a rebuttal of the polytheists based on the lack of evidence for the divinity of their gods.

His saying: **إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَما تَهْوى الْأَنْفُسُ** **"They follow nothing but conjectures and what their souls desire"** (53:23). The word "what" (ما) is a relative pronoun, and the returning pronoun is omitted, meaning "that which the soul desires." It is also said to be an infinitive (مصدرية), meaning "the desire of the soul." Desire (الهوى) is the lustful inclination of the soul. The sentence is intended to censure them for following falsehood and to emphasize what preceded—that they have no proof for their claims.

This is reinforced by His saying: **وَلَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدى** **"and certainly there has come to them guidance from their Lord"** (53:23). This sentence functions as a circumstantial clause (حالية).

The meaning is: These polytheists follow nothing regarding their gods except conjecture (الظن) and that which their souls lustfully incline toward, while the fact is that guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — has already come to them from Allah, Who is their Lord. This guidance is the true call or the Quran that guides them to the truth.

The shift (الالتفات) in the verse from direct address to the third person is to intimate that they are too low in understanding to be addressed with this speech, and that they are unprepared to be addressed with demonstrative speech (كلام برهاني) as they are followers of conjecture and desire.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ لِلْإِنْسانِ ما تَمَنَّى** **"Shall man have whatever he dreams of?"** (53:24). The word "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive, and the question is rhetorical. The speech is intended to negate that man possesses what he wishes for merely because he wishes for it. That is, man does not possess what he desires simply by desiring it, such that the polytheists would possess what they desire through the whims of their souls—namely, the intercession of the angels who are the lords of their idols and the daughters of Allah in their view—or that they would possess the divinity of their gods merely through wishing.

In this speech, there is an allusion that they have nothing to indicate the validity of the divinity of their gods or their intercession except wishing, and nothing is possessed through wishing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلِلَّهِ الْآخِرَةُ وَالْأُولى** **"To Allah belong the Hereafter and the present world."** (53:25). The branching of this from the preceding is the branching of the cause (العلة) from the effect (المعلول) to indicate connection and correlation; thus, it serves as a justification for the preceding sentence. The meaning is: Man does not possess what he wishes for merely by wishing, because the Hereafter and the present world belong to Allah, the Glorified, and He has no partner in His kingdom.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَمْ مِنْ مَلَكٍ فِي السَّماواتِ لا تُغْنِي شَفاعَتُهُمْ شَيْئاً إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ يَأْذَنَ اللهُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَرْضى** **"How many an angel there is in the heavens whose intercession is of no avail in any way except after Allah grants permission to whomever He wishes and approves!"** (53:26). The difference between permission (الإذن) and approval (الرضا) is that permission is the notification of the removal of an obstacle by the one permitting, while approval is the soul of the one approving being in harmony with the thing and not refusing it. Sometimes permission for something occurs without approval, but approval never occurs without permission, whether actual or potential.

The verse is intended to negate that the angels possess intercession of their own accord, independent of Allah, the Glorified, as the idol-worshippers intend. For the matter in its entirety belongs to Allah, the Exalted; only those among them who intercede do so after His permission for intercession and His approval of it.

Accordingly, what is meant by His saying: **لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** "to whomever He wishes" is the angels. The meaning of the verse is: Many of the angels in the heavens—their intercession has no effect except after Allah grants permission to whomever He wishes among them, i.e., the angels, and approves of his intercession.

It is also said that what is meant by "whomever He wishes and approves" is the human being. The meaning would then be: except after Allah permits the intercession for whomever He wishes to be interceded for among humans and approves. And how would He permit or approve intercession for one who disbelieved in Him and worshipped another?

The verse establishes intercession for the angels in general, but restricts their intercession to the permission and approval of Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ لَيُسَمُّونَ الْمَلائِكَةَ تَسْمِيَةَ الْأُنْثى** **"Indeed those who do not believe in the Hereafter give female names to the angels."** (53:27). This is a rebuttal of their claim regarding the femininity of the angels, following the rebuttal of their claim regarding their intercession.

What is meant by their "giving female names to the angels" is their saying that the angels are the daughters of Allah. Thus, "female" (الأنثى) refers to the gender, encompassing both the singular and the plural.

It is also said that "the angels" (الملائكة) carries the meaning of the exhaustion of the singular (استغراق المفرد), so the estimation would be: they name every single one of the angels with a female name, i.e., they call him a daughter. The speech follows the pattern of "The prince clothed us in a robe," meaning he clothed every one of us in a robe.

Some have said: Linking this naming to the lack of belief in the Hereafter intimates that it is so hideous and monstrous, and so consequential in terms of punishment in the Hereafter, that no one would dare commit it except one who does not believe in the Hereafter at all. [End quote].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَإِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً** **"They do not have any knowledge of that. They follow nothing but conjectures, and conjecture is no substitute for the truth."** (53:28). Knowledge (العلم) is the assent (التصديق) that precludes the opposite, while conjecture (الظن) is the preponderant assent, and the non-preponderant is called illusion (وهم). Their claim regarding the femininity of the angels was not something known to them, nor was it even conjectured, as there was no way to preponderate the claim over its opposite. However, since it stemmed from the desires of their souls, desire established it within them and beautified it for them, so they paid no heed to its opposite. Whenever a sign of its opposite appeared to them, they turned away from it and clung to what they desired. By this consideration, it is called "conjecture," though in reality, it is merely a conception (تصور).

From this, the soundness of the view of those who say that "conjecture" in this verse and in the previous saying—"They follow nothing but conjectures and what their souls desire"—means "imagination" (التوهم) rather than "preponderant belief" (الاعتقاد الراجح) becomes clear. This is supported by what appears in the words of al-Raghib: that conjecture is sometimes applied to imagination.

His saying: **إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً** "and conjecture is no substitute for the truth." The truth (الحق) is that which a thing is in itself. It is obvious that it cannot be perceived except through knowledge, which is the assent that precludes the opposite, and nothing else. As for things other than knowledge, which contain the possibility of the opposite, the perceived object is not determined as it is in reality. Therefore, it is not permissible to rely on it regarding fundamental truths. The Exalted said: **وَلا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ** "Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge" (17:36).

As for acting upon conjecture in practical rulings (الأحكام العملية), that is only due to the existence of evidence for it which restricts the generality of the verse, while matters of belief (الأمور الاعتقادية) remain under the generality of the verse.

Some have said: The explicit noun was placed in the position of the pronoun in His saying "Indeed, conjecture is no substitute" so that the speech would follow the course of a proverb.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا** **"So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world."** (53:29). This is branched from their following of conjecture and the desires of their souls. His saying "So avoid those..." is a command to turn away from them. He did not say "So avoid them," but instead placed the phrase "those who turn away from Our remembrance..." in the position of the pronoun to indicate the cause for the command to avoid them. It is as if it were said: These people abandon knowledge and follow conjecture and what their souls desire; they only did this because they turned away from the remembrance and desired the life of the world. Thus, they have no concern except the world, and it is the limit of their knowledge. Since this is the case, turn away from them because they are in error.

What is meant by "remembrance" (الذكر) is either the Quran, which guides its followers to the manifest truth and directs them to the happiness of the abode of the Hereafter—which lies beyond this world—through definitive arguments (الحجج القاطعة) and radiant proofs (البراهين الساطعة) that leave no stain of doubt.

Or it is the remembrance of Allah in the sense opposite to heedlessness (الغفلة). For remembering Him, the Exalted, with the names and attributes befitting His transcendent Essence, guides to all other scientific truths regarding the Origin and the Return with a scientific guidance (هداية علمية) that admits no doubt.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اهْتَدى** **"That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided."** (53:30). The word "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to the matter of the world, which is known from the preceding verse. Its being the "reach of their knowledge" is a type of metaphor (استعارة), as if knowledge travels toward the known and ends at it; their knowledge, in its journey, ended at the world, reached it, and stopped there without surpassing it. The necessary consequence of this is that the world becomes the object of their will and seeking, the locus of their concern, and the goal of their hopes; they find no tranquility in anything else and turn toward nothing but it.

His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ** "Indeed your Lord knows best..." is an emphasis of the content of the preceding sentence and a testimony from Him, the Exalted, regarding it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا بِما عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى** **"To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, that He may requite those who do evil for what they have done, and reward those who do good with the best reward."** (53:31). It is possible that the beginning of the verse is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of "knows best" in the preceding verse, with the *waw* being the *waw* of circumstance. The meaning would be: Your Lord knows best the two groups—the straying and the guided—while the fact is that He owns whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth; so how could He not know them when He is their Owner?

Accordingly, the phrase "that He may requite..." (لِيَجْزِيَ) would apparently be connected to His previous saying "So avoid those who turn away..." The meaning would be: Turn away from them and leave their matter to Allah, so that He may requite them with such-and-such, and requite you and the doers of good with such-and-such.

It is also possible that His saying "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens..." is a new sentence (كلام مستأنف) to indicate that the command to avoid them is not for the purpose of neglecting them or leaving them in vain; rather, Allah, the Glorified, requites everyone for their deed, whether evil or good. The use of the majestic name "Allah," which is an explicit noun, in place of a pronoun is to indicate perfect grandeur.

His saying: **لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" is an allusion to His ownership of everything. Its meaning is the subsistence of things through Him, the Exalted, because He is their Creator (الخالق) who brought them into existence. Thus, ownership arises from creation (الخلق), and it is simultaneously the source of governance (التدبير). So the sentence indicates both creation and governance, as if it were said: To Allah belong creation and governance.

In this sense, His saying "that He may requite..." is connected, with the *lam* indicating the final cause (الغاية). The meaning is: To Him belong creation and governance, and the end and purpose of that is that He may requite those who do evil, etc. What is meant by requital (الجزاء) is what the Book informs of regarding the affairs of the Day of Resurrection. What is meant by "doing evil" and "doing good" is disobedience and obedience. What is meant by "for what they have done" is the recompense for what they have done or the deeds themselves. By "the best reward" (الحسنى) is meant the beautiful reward.

The meaning is: That Allah may requite those who disobeyed for their disobedience or with the recompense for their disobedience, and requite those who obeyed with the beautiful reward. Other possibilities have been mentioned for the verse, but what we have presented is the most manifest.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَواحِشَ إِلَّا اللَّمَمَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ واسِعُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ** **"Those who avoid major sins and indecencies, excepting occasional slips. Indeed your Lord is expansive in forgiveness."** (53:32). Sin (الإثم) is an offense; its root—as mentioned by al-Raghib—is the act that slows one down from reward and goodness. Major sins (كبائر الإثم) are the great acts of disobedience, which, according to the narration (1), are those for which Allah has promised the Fire. The discussion regarding them preceded in the commentary on His saying: **إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31).

Indecencies (الفواحش) are the hideous and monstrous sins. He has counted adultery and sodomy among the indecencies in His speech, and it is not unlikely to infer from the verse that they are identical to the major sins.

As for "occasional slips" (اللمم), they have differed in its meaning. It is said to be the minor sins (الصغيرة); based on this, the exception is disjunctive (منقطع). It is also said to be when one approaches a sin and intends it but does not commit it; the exception would also be disjunctive. It is also said to be the sin committed from time to time without it being a habit—meaning a sin committed by chance. In this case, it would be more general than minor or major sins, and the content of the verse would correspond to the meaning of His saying, the Exalted, in describing the God-conscious doers of good: **وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا اللهَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَنْ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا عَلى ما فَعَلُوا وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** "and those who, when they commit an indecency or wrong themselves, remember Allah and plead for forgiveness for their sins—and who forgives sins except Allah?—and they do not persist in what they have committed while they know." (3:135).

It has been interpreted in the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) according to the third meaning (1).

The verse explains what was in the preceding verse: "those who do good." They are those who avoid major sins and indecencies, though it is possible for occasional slips to occur from them.

In His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ واسِعُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ** "Indeed your Lord is expansive in forgiveness," there is an encouragement for them to repent in hope of forgiveness.

His saying: **هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ** "He knows you best since He brought you forth from the earth" (53:32). Al-Raghib said: Bringing forth (النشء والنشأة) is the origination of a thing and its nurturing. [End quote]. So His bringing them forth from the earth refers to what occurred to them at the beginning of their creation, stage after stage, from their being taken from elemental materials until they were formed into the state of sperm and entered the wombs.

His saying: **وَإِذْ أَنْتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِي بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ** "and since you were fetuses in the bellies of your mothers" (53:32). Fetuses (الأجنة) is the plural of *janin*. The speech is coordinated with the preceding "since" (إِذْ), meaning: He knows you best when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers; He knows what your reality is, what state you are in, what is in your secret, and what your matter will result in.

His saying: **فَلا تُزَكُّوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** "so do not flaunt your purity" (53:32) is branched from the knowledge. That is: since Allah knows best from the very beginning, do not flaunt your purity by attributing yourselves to sanctity; He knows best who is God-conscious.

***

(1) Narrated in *Thawab al-A'mal* from 'Abbad ibn Kathir al-Nawa from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).
(1) In *Usul al-Kafi* from Ibn 'Ammar from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The slip (اللم) is the man who approaches a sin and then seeks forgiveness from Allah for it." And in it, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "It is the sin a man approaches, then remains as long as Allah wills, then approaches it again." And in it, with its chain to Ibn 'Ammar from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The one who approaches sin after sin, it is not of his nature (سليقته)," meaning his disposition.
